items.prompt_md: Python's `heapq` only implements a min-heap. How does the last-stone-weight solution track the running **maximum**?
options: It negates every value when pushing and negates again when popping
It keeps a separate variable tracking the max alongside the heap
It re-heapifies the whole list after every round
items.answer: It negates every value when pushing and negates again when popping
items.explanation_md: Storing `-value` makes the largest original value the smallest stored value, so it lands at the min-heap's root. Negate again on the way out to recover the real value.

items.prompt_md: You repeatedly need the two currently-largest elements, combine them, and may feed a new value back into the same pool. Which approach fits?
options: Sort once at the start and index into the sorted list
Use a heap: pop the top one or two elements, push back any derived value
Use a hash map counting the frequency of each value
Use two pointers from both ends of a sorted array
items.answer: Use a heap: pop the top one or two elements, push back any derived value
items.explanation_md: A round-based "pop the current extremum, maybe push a derived value back" process is exactly what a heap is built for — each round costs O(log n) regardless of how the rest of the pool is arranged.

items.prompt_md: With n stones, what is the total time complexity of repeatedly popping the two heaviest and pushing back the difference until at most one stone remains?
items.explanation_md: Each round does O(1) pops and at most one push, each costing O(log n); there are O(n) rounds since the pool shrinks by at least one stone per round, giving O(n log n) total.
